title: Italic proportions — extended and compressed
sources: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906) — public domain; Italic hand analysis. Alfred Fairbank, A Handwriting Manual (1932) — copyright (Fairbank died 1982); structural description only.
notes: Same Italic hand, two width settings. Standard Italic: body width roughly 5 nib-widths across the o. Extended Italic: body width 6-7 nib-widths — the o is nearly round, the letters open and spreading. Compressed Italic: body width 3-4 nib-widths — the o is narrow, letters tall and condensed, reading vertical even with 5-8° slope. Covers: how to shift between the two by adjusting the o model (start with the o then carry the width through the whole alphabet), spacing adjustments (compressed letters need slightly less interspace; extended letters need slightly more), where each proportion fits — extended for headings and display, compressed for text blocks where horizontal space is limited.
